message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://findskill.ai/blog/best-open-source-tts-2026/
**Title:** Best Open-Source TTS 2026: Chatterbox 65.3% Beats ElevenLabs
**Found via:** academic/benchmark — MOS, license, model size search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- cloudflare-one: Guides Cloudflare One Zero Trust and SASE work across Access, Gateway, WARP, Tunnel, Cloudflare WAN, DLP, CASB, device posture, and identity. Use when designing, configuring, troubleshooting, or reviewing Cloudflare One deployments. Retrieval-first: use current Cloudflare docs/API schemas instead of embedded product docs.
- cloudflare-one-migrations: Plans migrations from Zscaler ZIA/ZPA, Palo Alto, legacy VPN, SWG, or SASE stacks to Cloudflare One. Use for migration assessments, policy mapping, rollout plans, and parity/gap analysis.
- durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- turnstile-spin: Set up Cloudflare Turnstile end-to-end in a project — scan the codebase, create the widget via the Cloudflare API, deploy the managed siteverify Worker, write the frontend snippets, validate, and persist the skill. Load this when a user asks to add Turnstile, set up CAPTCHA, protect a form from bots, or fix a Turnstile integration. Mirrors developers.cloudflare.com/turnstile/spin.
- web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the page content and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract all concrete, falsifiable claims about open-source/local TTS models relevant to: quality/naturalness (MOS scores, benchmarks), licenses (commercial/redistribution), model sizes, word/phoneme-level timing/alignment output, intelligibility under speed/time-compression, multi-voice/voice cloning/prosody control, and local inference practicality (Apple Silicon, CPU, RAM/VRAM, real-time factor). Models of interest: Kokoro-82M, Chatterbox, XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Kitten-TTS, Zonos. Quote the exact text for each claim. Also report the publish date of the article and note whether this is independent benchmarking or vendor marketing.

content.content:
# Extractive Summary: TTS Model Claims from FindSkill.ai Article

**Article Publish Date:** March 28, 2026 (Updated May 15, 2026)

**Classification:** Vendor marketing + community consensus synthesis (cites r/LocalLLaMA, includes vendor-run blind test)

---

## Concrete, Falsifiable Claims Extracted

### **Kokoro-82M**
- **Model size:** "82M" parameters
- **Languages:** "8 (English + Japanese, Chinese, several European)"
- **Real-time performance:** "90-210× real-time on decent hardware"
- **Memory:** "~2-3 GB VRAM at FP16; weights under 1 GB"
- **Hardware:** "runs on CPU and Mac M-series perfectly well"
- **Voice cloning:** "No zero-shot cloning"
- **Voices:** "54 presets"
- **License:** "Open (check repo for current terms)"

### **Chatterbox-Turbo**
- **Model size:** "350M (Turbo); ~500M (full Chatterbox)"
- **Languages:** "23+ (via MTL training)"
- **Voice cloning:** "~5-10 seconds of reference audio"
- **Latency:** "~75 ms, **6× real-time** on a single consumer GPU"
- **Hardware:** "Single consumer GPU (RTX 30/40-series comfortable, 4-16 GB VRAM)"
- **License:** "MIT"
- **Blind test claim:** "**65.3% of listeners preferred Chatterbox-Turbo, 24.5% preferred ElevenLabs**"
- **Features:** "Paralinguistic tags `[laugh]`, pacing controls, emotion control"

### **Coqui XTTS v2**
- **Languages:** "17 (including Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Arabic, Turkish, Russian, Polish)"
- **Voice cloning:** "6 seconds of reference audio"
- **Hardware:** "8GB GPU"
- **License:** "MPL 2.0 (commercial use allowed with conditions)"
- **Status:** "Coqui (the company) shut down in 2024"

### **Piper**
- **Languages:** "30+ (widest coverage)"
- **Hardware:** "CPU only — no GPU needed. Runs on Raspberry Pi, Android, embedded Linux"
- **Voice cloning:** "No"
- **License:** "MIT"

### **Voxtral TTS (Mistral)**
- **Parameters:** "4B"
- **Languages:** "9" (English, French, German, Spanish, Dutch, Portuguese, Italian, Hindi, Arabic)
- **Voice cloning:** "3 seconds of reference audio"
- **Latency:** "90ms time-to-first-audio"
- **Hardware:** "3GB RAM minimum, 16GB GPU recommended"
- **License:** "Creative Commons"
- **Blind test claim:** "62.8% of listeners preferred Voxtral over ElevenLabs Flash v2.5"

### **MetaVoice**
- **Languages:** "English only"
- **Voice cloning:** "30 seconds"
- **Hardware:** "8GB GPU"
- **License:** "Apache 2.0"
- **Status:** "acquired by ElevenLabs in 2024"

### **CosyVoice2-0.5B**
- **Parameters:** "500M (0.5B)"
- **Languages:** "9 base (Chinese, English, Japanese, Korean, German, Spanish, French, Italian, Russian) + 18+ Chinese dialects"
- **Latency:** "~150 ms streaming first-packet"
- **Hardware:** "~8 GB VRAM (runs on Jetson-class edge devices)"
- **Error reduction:** "30-50% fewer pronunciation errors vs CosyVoice1"

### **Bark (Suno)**
- **Languages:** "13+"
- **Hardware:** "12GB GPU recommended"
- **Voice cloning:** "Not built-in"
- **License:** "MIT"
- **Feature:** "Bark generates not just speech but laughter, sighs, music, and environmental sounds"

---

## Models NOT Found in Article
- StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Kitten-TTS, Zonos: **No claims extracted** (not mentioned)

---

## Assessment
- **MOS scores / independent benchmarks:** None provided (only vendor-run blind test)
- **Word/phoneme timing output:** Not discussed
- **Intelligibility under compression:** Not addressed
- **Apple Silicon specifics:** Only Kokoro mentioned "Mac M-series"

content.text: The fetch succeeded. The article is a blog post that mixes vendor marketing with community-consensus synthesis, citing a vendor-run blind test rather than independent academic benchmarks. Let me record my assessment.

claims.claim: Kokoro-82M (the baseline) is an 82M-parameter model that runs well on Apple Silicon (Mac M-series) and CPU, achieving 90-210x real-time, needing under 1GB weights / ~2-3GB VRAM at FP16, offering 54 voice presets but no zero-shot voice cloning.
claims.quote: 82M ... 90-210× real-time on decent hardware ... ~2-3 GB VRAM at FP16; weights under 1 GB ... runs on CPU and Mac M-series perfectly well ... No zero-shot cloning ... 54 presets

claims.claim: In the article's blind test, 65.3% of listeners preferred Chatterbox-Turbo while 24.5% preferred ElevenLabs; Chatterbox is MIT-licensed, supports ~5-10s reference voice cloning, paralinguistic tags and emotion control, and runs ~6x real-time on a single consumer GPU (4-16GB VRAM).
claims.quote: 65.3% of listeners preferred Chatterbox-Turbo, 24.5% preferred ElevenLabs ... License: MIT ... ~5-10 seconds of reference audio ... ~75 ms, 6× real-time on a single consumer GPU ... Paralinguistic tags [laugh], pacing controls, emotion control

claims.claim: Coqui XTTS v2 supports 17 languages and 6-second voice cloning under an MPL 2.0 license (commercial use allowed with conditions), needs an 8GB GPU, and the originating company Coqui shut down in 2024.
claims.quote: 17 (including Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Arabic, Turkish, Russian, Polish) ... 6 seconds of reference audio ... 8GB GPU ... MPL 2.0 (commercial use allowed with conditions) ... Coqui (the company) shut down in 2024

claims.claim: Piper is an MIT-licensed, CPU-only TTS (no GPU needed; runs on Raspberry Pi, Android, embedded Linux) with the widest language coverage (30+) but no voice cloning.
claims.quote: 30+ (widest coverage) ... CPU only — no GPU needed. Runs on Raspberry Pi, Android, embedded Linux ... Voice cloning: No ... License: MIT

claims.claim: The article provides no MOS scores, no independent academic benchmarks, no word/phoneme-level timing/alignment data, and no high-speed-compression intelligibility evaluation; quality claims rest on a vendor-run blind test and community consensus (r/LocalLLaMA).
claims.quote: MOS scores / independent benchmarks: None provided (only vendor-run blind test) ... Word/phoneme timing output: Not discussed ... Intelligibility under compression: Not addressed

content.text:
Extracted claims from the FindSkill.ai blog post. Summary of my assessment:

**Source quality: blog** — This is a marketing/SEO-oriented blog post (findskill.ai) that synthesizes community consensus (r/LocalLLaMA) and cites a vendor-run blind test rather than independent academic benchmarking. The headline "65.3% Beats ElevenLabs" derives from a vendor blind test, not a peer-reviewed MOS study, so quality preference figures should be treated as marketing-adjacent rather than authoritative.

**Publish date:** 2026-03-28 (updated 2026-05-15).

**Relevance to the research question:** Moderate. It directly addresses the Kokoro baseline (size, Apple Silicon/CPU feasibility, real-time factor, 54 voices, no cloning) and offers license/size/voice-cloning data for Chatterbox, XTTS-v2, Piper, plus a few others (Voxtral, CosyVoice2, Bark, MetaVoice). However it has notable gaps for this pipeline: it does **not** cover StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Kitten-TTS, or Zonos, and provides **no** data on word/phoneme-level timing/alignment, forced-alignment tooling, or intelligibility under aggressive time-compression — three of the five core angles. Apple-Silicon specifics are limited to Kokoro.

The most load-bearing extracted facts (Kokoro baseline profile, Chatterbox MIT license + cloning + the contested 65.3% preference figure, XTTS-v2 MPL-2.0 commercial terms, Piper MIT/CPU-only) are captured in the structured output above.
